coredata.dc:title: Graphitic carbon nitride decorated with S,N co-doped graphene quantum dots for enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Aijun
Wang, Qian
Chang, Yongfang
Wang, Xiuping
coredata.dc:description: S,N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) were successfully combined with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) in a facile manner by a physical method. The as-prepared g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites were characterized by X-ray diffraction, high-resolution field-emission scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, diffuse-reflectance spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and other techniques. The g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites exhibit higher photocatalytic performance for degrading Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible-light irradiation than the pure g-C3N4. The enhancement of photodegradation activity is attributed to the efficient separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. Moreover, these composites can be reused for the degradation of RhB during nine cycles, without a significant decrease in the photocatalytic activity. Finally, a possible charge separation mechanism was proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites
Visible light photodegradation
Mechanism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51402087 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province ( C2014407008 ), the Program for the Top Young Talents of Higher Learning Institutions of Hebei Province ( BJ2014026 ), and the PhD research Startup Foundation of Hebei University of Science & Technology, China ( 2014YB002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of the S,N:GQDs (inset shows the HRTEM image of a particle), (b) TEM image of g-C3N4, (c) TEM image of sample 3, and (d) HRTEM image of the area indicated by the blue rectangle in (c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XRD patterns of the S,N:GQDs, g-C3N4, and g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites.
FTIR spectra of the g-C3N4, S,N:GQDs, and g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites.
XPS spectra of (a) C1s and (b) N1s for sample 3.
UV–Vis DRS of the S,N:GQDs, g-C3N4, and g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites.
PL spectra of the g-C3N4 and various g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites.
(a) Photodegradation of RhB with different samples under visible-light irradiation, and (b) kinetic study of the degradation of the RhB in the presence of the various samples.
(a) Stability study for the photocatalytic RhB degradation by sample 3, and (b) XRD patterns of before and after nine consecutive photocatalytic reactions.
Proposed mechanism of the photocatalytic degradation of RhB for the g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites.

head.ce:title: Graphitic carbon nitride decorated with S,N co-doped graphene quantum dots for enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science and Technology, Hebei Normal University of Science & Technology, Qinhuangdao 066600, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Science and Technology
Hebei Normal University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Langfang Teachers University, Langfang 065000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Technology, Shijiazhuang University, Shijianzhuang 050035, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: S,N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) were successfully combined with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) in a facile manner by a physical method. The as-prepared g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites were characterized by X-ray diffraction, high-resolution field-emission scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, diffuse-reflectance spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and other techniques. The g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites exhibit higher photocatalytic performance for degrading Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible-light irradiation than the pure g-C3N4. The enhancement of photodegradation activity is attributed to the efficient separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. Moreover, these composites can be reused for the degradation of RhB during nine cycles, without a significant decrease in the photocatalytic activity. Finally, a possible charge separation mechanism was proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: As a promising metal-free photocatalyst, polymeric graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted significant interest, caused by its abundance, non-toxicity, and structural stability [1,2]. It exhibits good photocatalytic activity for the water splitting and the degradation of environmental pollutants under visible light irradiation. However, the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4 is restricted because of rapid charge recombination [3–5] and the low surface area, which lead to the inefficiency in utilization of solar energy [6,7]. Thus far, numerous efforts have been devoted for the improvement of the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4, such as semiconductor compositing [8,9], metal-non-metal doping [10–12], and nano-mesostructures fabrication [13,14]. Nevertheless, attaining efficient durable charge separation is still a significant challenge.

Carbon-modified semiconductors have been reported to accelerate electron transfer, caused by the unique electron transport ability of carbon [15–17]. Carbon materials have been used to enhance the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4. For example, Hao et al. have fabricated carbonized polydopamine-g-C3N4, which exhibited significant visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity for hydrogen production [18]. Lu et al. have prepared graphene oxide-g-C3N4 hybrids by a sonication-assisted method [19]. As compared with pure g-C3N4 nanosheets, the hybrid exhibit enhanced photocatalytic activity toward the photodegradation of methylene blue.

Quantum dots (QDs) are zero-dimensional semiconductors that have been widely used in many fields across the physical, chemical, and biological sciences [20–22]. As a type kind of QDs, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be regarded as very tiny pieces of graphene with few layers to tens of layers [23,24]. As compared with other semiconductor QDs, GQDs exhibit good electrical conductivity, tunable band gaps, and extraordinary optoelectronic properties. Thus, they have been applied in several fields, such as in photocatalysis [25], ion detection [26], bioimaging [27,28], and electrochemical biosensing [29]. The optical and electrical properties of GQDs can be tuned by doping with S and/or N [30,31]. Hence, it can be deduced that S,N co-doped GQDs (S,N:GQDs) may exhibit more extensive applications in catalysis. However, to our knowledge, no study has been published on the use of g-C3N4/S,N:GQDs photocatalysts for the degradation of organic pollutants under visible light irradiation.

Herein, we prepare S,N:GQDs decorated in situ on g-C3N4 nanosheets for the fabrication of unique g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites, by a simple one-pot physical method. The as-prepared composites possess excellent visible-light photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation of RhB, caused by the efficient charge separation and high transfer efficiency of photogenerated electrons. Furthermore, the g-C3N4/S,N:GQD photocatalysts exhibit excellent stability without a significant loss in activity after nine cycles.

First, S,N:GQDs were synthesized by a modified procedure reported in literature [32]. Typically, 13.1 mmol citric acid and 36.3 mmol thiourea were first dissolved into 60 mL deionized water under stirring to obtain a homogeneous solution. Second, the mixture was then transferred into a Teflon autoclave (100 mL) and was heated at 160 °C for 18 h. Next, after cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation, followed by washing several times with absolute alcohol. Finally, the S,N:GQDs were freeze-dried and redissolved in 30 mL deionized water to form a green solution.
The g-C3N4 sample was obtained by directly heating urea at 500 °C for 3 h in air at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The resultant yellow products were then collected and milled into a fine powder.

First, a specified volume of a S,N:GQD solution was withdrawn from the above-prepared S,N:GQD solution and was then mixed with 0.1 g g-C3N4 under vigorously stirring. The total volume of mixture was maintained at 30 mL by adding deionized water. Second, the mixture was stirred for 24 h at room temperature. Third, the products were collected by centrifugation, washed three times with deionized water and dried overnight at 60 °C. Hereafter, the products are named as sample 1, sample 2and sample 3 with 7.5 mL, 15 mL and 30 mL of the S, N:GQD solution added, respectively.

To increase the percentage of S, N:GQDs in the composites, the hydrothermal method was also used. Typically, 0.1 g g-C3N4 was first added into 30 mL of the S,N:GQD solution. Second, the mixture was continuously stirred for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous suspension. Subsequently, the suspension was transferred into a Teflon-sealed autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 5 h. The products obtained were named as sample 4.

The morphology of the as-prepared products was studied by field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi S4800) with an energy-dispersive spectrometer (EDS) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN microscope). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8-ADVANCE X-ray powder diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on an ESCALAB 250 instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using KBr pellets with a Nicolet Magna-IR 750 spectrometer. Elemental analyses (EA) for the carbon and nitrogen contents were investigated on a Vario Micro cube CHN analyzer. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was conducted on a Hitachi U-4100 spectrophotometer. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) analysis of the products were performed using a Micromeritics 3Flex surface characterization analyzer. Zeta potential of the sample was determined using 3000HS Particle size and Zeta Potential Analyzer.

Electrochemical analysis was conducted using an electrochemical system (CHI-660B, China), with a conventional three-electrode cell. A Pt wire and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The electrolyte was a 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution. The working electrode was prepared on FTO glass (13 mm × 10 mm), which was cleaned by sonication in acetone for 30 min and dried at 353 K. The boundary of FTO glass was covered with an insulating tape, with the exposed effective area measuring 1 cm2. Typically, 10 mg of the products was dispersed in 2 mL ethanol and then ground to obtain a slurry. Next, the slurry was spread onto the pretreated FTO glass and was dried at 393 K. The variation of the photoinduced current density with time (i-t curve) was measured at a bias potential of 0 V (vs SCE) during five cycles when light was switched on and off. A 500 W Xe lamp with a D300 monochromator was used to provide a light irradiation of 254 nm.

The photocatalytic activity of the g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites was evaluated with RhB. A 500 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter was used as the visible light source. The distance between the lamp and suspension was 15 cm. Typically, 50 mg of catalysts was dispersed in an aqueous RhB solution (100 mL, 5 mg/L) and magnetically stirred in the dark for 1 h to attain the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Next, the mixture was then illuminated under visible light. Then, at certain time intervals, a 4 mL suspension was withdrawn from the reaction and subjected to centrifugation. The RhB concentration was monitored using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 553 nm.

As shown in Fig. S1, the as-prepared S,N:GQD solution exhibits a homogeneous phase at room temperature and is yellow-green. The morphology of the S,N:GQDs was investigated by TEM. Fig. 1 a shows the TEM image of the S,N:GQDs. The S,N:GQDs are monodisperse with a fairly uniform diameter of approximately 6–10 nm. The S,N:GQDs exhibit a narrow size distribution (Fig. S2). A representative HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1a) displays a lattice spacing distance of 0.24 nm, which is in good agreement with the (1120) plane of graphite. XPS confirms the presence of the elemental S and N (Fig. S3). g-C3N4 was also determined by TEM. As can be observed in Fig. 1b, the g-C3N4 possesses a two-dimensional agglomerated layered lamellar-like morphology, due to π-π interaction. Fig. 1c shows the TEM image of the sample 3. The g-C3N4 nanosheets still exhibit large cross-linking structures. The g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites are clearly observed. HRTEM was conducted to investigate the area marked by the blue rectangle in Fig. 1c, and Fig. 1d shows the results. HRTEM observation further confirms that the composites are composed of g-C3N4 and S,N:GQDs. A lattice spacing of 0.32 nm corresponds to the distance between the (002) crystal planes of g-C3N4. A lattice spacing of 0.24 nm corresponds to the interplanar distance of the (1120) lattice planes of S,N:GQDs. With increasing S,N:GQD content in the composites, the optical change of the composites is clearly observed (Fig. S4). As can be observed in Fig. S4, sample 3 shows the same color as yellow g-C3N4, while sample 4 becomes light gray, caused by the increasing adsorption of the S,N:GQDs adsorbed on the g-C3N4 surface.

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of the S,N:GQDs, g-C3N4, and various g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites. The S,N:GQDs exhibit a broad and strong diffraction peak at around 23.6°. The formation of a graphite-like stacking g-C3N4 layer is also confirmed. The peak at 13.1° corresponding to the in-pane structural packing motif of tristriazine units is attributed to the (100) crystal plane [33]. Another intense peak around 27.5° corresponding to the interlayer stacking of the aromatic units of CN is indexed to the (002) plane of the stacking of the conjugated aromatic systems [34,35]. After the combination of the S,N:GQDs and g-C3N4, the peak of g-C3N4 at 13.1° decreases with the addition of S,N:GQDs, while the (002) peak of S, N:GQDs appears at 10.5°. The results indicate that the S,N:GQDs are successfully drafted on the g-C3N4 surface.

Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of the g-C3N4, S,N:GQDs, and a series of g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites. For g-C3N4, three main absorption regions are clearly observed. The broad band in the range of the 3000-3500 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of N-H and stretching vibration of OH of the physically adsorbed water [36]. The strong band of 1200–1700 cm−1, with the characteristic the peaks at 1634, 1408, 1327, and 1246 cm−1 are ascribed to the typical stretching modes of CN heterocycles [37]. The peak at 808 cm−1 is attributed to the s-triazine ring modes [38]. For the S,N:GQDs, the broad peaks at 3000-3500 cm−1 are ascribed to OH and NH stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1707, 1664, 1576, and 1400 cm−1 belong to the CO vibration in COOH, CO vibration in CONH, CC bending vibration, and C-N bending vibration, respectively. The peaks at 1109 and 632 cm−1 is related to CS and C-S stretching, respectively [32]. The FTIR spectra of the g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites represent the overlap of the spectra of both g-C3N4 and S,N:GQDs, which suggests the successful combination of the g-C3N4 and S,N:GQDs. Notably, the intensity of the peaks at 1109 and 632 cm−1 increases with increasing S,N:GQDs content.

Fig. S5 shows the Raman spectra of g-C3N4, S,N:GQDs and sample 3. S,N:GQDs show the disordered (D) band at 1371 cm−1, related to the presence of sp3 defects, and the crystalline (G) band at 1571 cm−1, assigned to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon. The Raman spectrum of sample 3 is similar with that of pure g-C3N4, due to the low loading of S,N:GQDs on the surface of g-C3N4.

To determine the different element contents in the g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites, EA was carried out. Table 1 lists the elemental analysis results of the pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites. The atomic ratio of carbon to nitrogen in pure g-C3N4 is 0.56. The atomic ratio of carbon to nitrogen in samples 1, 2, 3, and 4 is improved to 0.57, 0.58, 0.59, and 0.60, respectively, which originates form the loading of S,N:GQDs.

XPS spectroscopy was employed to reveal the valence states of various species of the g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites. As shown in Fig. S6, the full scan XPS spectra of the sample 3 present the signals of elemental C, N and O. Fig. 4 a shows the high-resolution scan of C 1s. The peak at 284.7 eV is assigned to adventitious carbon species, and the peak at 288.1 eV is assigned to sp3-bonded C in CN of g-C3N4 [39–41]. The N 1s peak XPS spectrum exhibits three peaks at 398.8, 400.1, and 404.3 eV (Fig. 4b), which are attributed to the C-N-C, tertiary nitrogen N-(C)3, and the charging effects, respectively [42,43].

To evaluate the electronic interaction between g-C3N4 and S,N:GQDs, photocurrent measurements were conducted for sample 3, g-C3N4, and S,N:GQDs electrodes (Fig. 5 ). As can be seen from Fig. 5, the photocurrent intensity of the sample 3 is significantly higher than that of either g-C3N4 or S,N:GQDs. The photocurrent enhancement of g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites results from a higher separation efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs and a lower recombination rate [44].

Fig. 6 shows the UV–Vis DRS of the S,N:GQDs, g-C3N4, and g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites. The S,N:GQDs exhibit visible light absorption more broad than the g-C3N4. As compared with that of pure g-C3N4, the reflectance of the various g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites decreases in visible light region. The results show that the visible-light absorption of the g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites increases because of the presence of S,N:GQDs on the g-C3N4 surface. Hence, S,N:GQDs play a critical role in the enhanced visible light absorption of the composites, which is expected to improve their visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity.

Fig. 7 shows the PL spectra of pure g-C3N4 and a series of g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites. Pure g-C3N4 exhibits an emission peak centered at approximately 442 nm. However, sample 2 and sample 3 display obvious PL quenching. This result suggests that a certain amount of loaded S, N:GQDs can highly improve the separation efficiency of the photogenerated electrons and holes, because PL emission originates from the recombination of the excited electron-hole pairs. The S,N:GQDs can efficiently enhance the visible-light utilization for g-C3N4. In particular, the PL peak of sample 3 shows the lowest intensity, which is in agreement with the result obtained from photocatalysis.

The photocatalytic activity of all photocatalysts was investigated for the removal of RhB under visible light irradiation. The degradation efficiency of the samples can be defined as follows:
(1) Degradation (%) = (1−C/C0) × 100%
where C and C
0 represent the RhB concentration at time t and the equilibrium concentration of dye, respectively.

The kinetics of degradation of RhB was also investigated. The experimental data are fitted with the pseudo first-order-kinetic equation as expressed by Eq. (2).
(2) ln(C/C
0 ) = −kt
where k is the apparent reaction rate constant (min−1).

Pre-experiments have demonstrated that RhB is stable and cannot be degraded over 90 min in the absence of the catalysts. Fig. 8 a shows the photodegradation of RhB with different photocatalysts. After irradiation for 90 min, no obvious degradation of RhB is observed in the presence of the S,N:GQDs, which indicates that S,N:GQDs exhibit no photocatalytic activity under visible light. On the other hand, after the same photoirradiation time, pure g-C3N4 can degrade RhB by 76%. Although various g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites can degrade the RhB solution, the degradation degree of RhB does not increase with increasing S,N:GQDs amount in composites. Sample 3 exhibits higher photocatalytic activity than other photocatalysts and can degrade 96% RhB within 90 min, which is indicative of the superior photocatalytic performance of sample 3. Its apparent rate constant is approximately 2.6 times greater that of the pure g-C3N4 (Fig. 8b), which indicates the coupling effects of S,N:GQDs and g-C3N4.

To evaluate the stability of the g-C3N4/S,N:GQD photocatalysts, recycling experiment was investigated for the photodegradation of RhB over sample 3. As can be seen from Fig. 9 a, no obvious loss in the photocatalytic activity is observed after nine cycles. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 9b, the XRD patterns of sample 3 are the same before and after nine consecutive cycles, suggest that the composite is stable during the reactions. These results indicate that g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites are excellent recyclable photocatalysts with high efficiency.

The effect of initial solution on the degradation efficiency of RhB was studied in the pH range of 4.0–9.0 in the presence of sample 3 after 90 min visible-light irradiation. As seen in Fig. S7, the degradation efficiency increases with increase in pH and reaches the maximum value of 100% at pH 7.0. The degradation efficiency of a catalyst is influenced by the adsorption effect between dye molecules and catalyst. The zero point of the sample 3 was determined and is shown in Fig. S8. The isoelectric point (IEP) for sample 3 is near 2.5, and above this value the sample 3 surface is negatively charged. Moreover, with the increased pH, the negative charge on the sample 3 increases. Because RhB is a kind of typical cationic dye, negative charge on the catalyst should contribute to increase adsorption of RhB on the catalyst. However, the activity of sample 3 is inhibited when the pH value is above 7. The result may originate from the competition between hydroxyl ions and dye molecules in the adsorption on the surface of catalyst, which hinders the formation of hydroxyl radical (·OH) [22].

The amount of catalysts plays an important role in degradation of dye solution. Fig. S9 shows the degradation efficiency of RhB solution in the presence of different amount of sample 3. It is observed that the photodegradation efficiency increases with the increase of the amount of sample 3. The degradation efficiencies of RhB are 20.3, 73, 96, and 97.7% for the sample 3 at catalyst loadings of 0.01, 0.02 0.05 and 0.1 g, respectively. The photocatalytic process is influenced by the availability of active sites on the surface of catalyst [45]. Usually, increasing the catalyst loading can increase the total active surface. However, it is interesting that a slowing growth of degradation efficiency is observed, when the loading amount of sample 3 increases from 0.05 g to 0.1 g. This result originates from the decrease in visible-light penetration with excess catalyst loading, thus leading to the decrease of photoactivated volume of suspension [46].

The photodegradation of RhB solution was investigated at different initial concentration of RhB solution. Fig. S10 shows that the degradation efficiency decreases with the increase of RhB concentration from 2.5 to 20 mg/mL. The degradation efficiency of the catalyst is related with the formation of OH on the catalyst surface and probability of OH reacting with dye molecules [46]. The formation of OH needs a large amount of photons entering the solution. The path length of the photons entering the solution decreases with the increase of the initial concentration of RhB solution, which can decrease the photon absorption by the catalyst.

The photocatalytic activity is well known to be closely related to the phase structure, adsorption ability, BET surface area, and the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes [38,47]. As seen from the XRD results, the crystal phase structure of g-C3N4 has not evidently changed after the combination with g-C3N4/S,N:GQDs. The adsorption ability can be determined by the adsorption experiment under the dark conditions. As seen from Fig. S11, the S,N:GQDs do not evidently adsorb RhB. As compared with pure g-C3N4, all g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites exhibit the similar adsorption ability. In addition, as shown in Fig. S12, sample 3 has lower BET surface area (45.53 m2/g) than g-C3N4 (52.99 m2/g), might due to the blocking of the holes of pure g-C3N4 by S,N:GQDs.

As discussed above, the enhancement in the photocatalytic activity of the g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites should mainly depend on the enhanced charge separation efficiency, which is confirmed by PL and photocurrent measurements. Fig. 10 shows a scheme for electron-hole separation and transport at the interface of the visible-light-driven g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites. GQDs can absorb visible light and then emit short wavelength light that is absorbed by g-C3N4 [48]. Meanwhile, g-C3N4 also can directly absorb visible light. First, the photoinduced electrons (e−) transfer from the valence band of g-C3N4 to the conduction band. Because of the interfacial contact formed between g-C3N4 and S,N:GQDs, the photogenerated electrons can be transferred to the S, N:GQDs for effective collection. This process hinders the recombination rates of the electron-hole pairs, which leads to the effective charge separation. The excited e− stored in S,N:GQDs are trapped by O2 molecules to produce the superoxide radicals (·O2 −). In addition, abundant holes (h+) are left on the valence band of g-C3N4, which can be trapped by surface hydroxyl groups or adsorbed water to produce OH. Both O2 − and OH are active species to effectively decompose the organic pollutant such as RhB.

In summary, a series of visible-light-driven g-C3N4/S,N:GQD photocatalysts are synthesized by a facile physical method. The introduction of S,N:GQDs results in the improvement of several properties of g-C3N4/S,N:GQD composites, such as optical, photocurrent, and catalytic properties. The remarkable enhancement in photocatalytic activity is mainly attributed to the efficient photogenerated electron-hole separation, caused by the combination between S,N:GQDs and g-C3N4. Furthermore, the g-C3N4/S,N:GQD photocatalysts exhibit excellent stability without significant loss in activity after nine repeated runs. The results provided here shed new insight for the fabrication of highly efficient and stable photocatalysts for the degradation of organic pollutants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51402087), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (C2014407008), the Program for the Top Young Talents of Higher Learning Institutions of Hebei Province (BJ2014026), and the PhD research Startup Foundation of Hebei University of Science & Technology, China (2014YB002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2016.09.030.
